headline: Canada Taps Into Natural Hydrogen Hidden Beneath Its Soil
meta_description: Canadian scientists discover natural hydrogen gas flowing from underground rocks, offering a clean energy source that could power homes and industries.
summary_short: Researchers in Canada have discovered that ancient rocks naturally produce hydrogen gas—a clean energy source that may be cheaper and more accessible than expected.
body:
A team of Canadian geologists has made an unexpected discovery: the rocks beneath our feet are quietly producing hydrogen gas, and there's plenty of it.

While studying an active mine near Timmins, Ontario, researchers found that boreholes drilled by gold miners release about 8 kilograms of hydrogen gas annually. With nearly 15,000 boreholes across the site, the total output exceeds 140 metric tonnes per year—enough to generate approximately 4.7 million kilowatts of energy, meeting the yearly needs of more than 400 homes.

The discovery goes further. In Saskatchewan, exploration company MAX Power drilled 1.2 miles through prairie bedrock and struck what the company calls a "discovered geological system." The sample returned a 28% hydrogen concentration, remarkably free of toxic hydrogen sulphide.

"Natural hydrogen is produced over time through underground chemical reactions between rocks and groundwaters," explains Professor Barbara Sherwood Lollar of the University of Toronto. "Canada is blessed that vast amounts of its territories contain the right rocks and minerals to create this natural hydrogen."

Unlike industrial hydrogen production—which relies on fossil fuels and releases carbon emissions—this "white hydrogen" flows naturally from the earth. It's a resource that could help Canada power remote communities and industries while reducing dependence on imported fuels. The discovery suggests that similar hydrogen-producing rocks may exist worldwide, opening possibilities for clean energy far beyond Canada's borders.
